title: Netflix — Video Streaming System Design
description: Designing a video-on-demand streaming platform: ingest and transcode huge video files, distribute them globally through CDNs, and stream them to any device at the best quality the network allows. The interesting problems are media-specific: an asynchronous upload/transcoding pipeline producing a bitrate ladder, CDN/edge distribution to minimize latency and bandwidth cost, adaptive bitrate streaming so playback adjusts to changing networks, and resilient playback sessions. We build from a naive 'serve the file' toward a transcoded, CDN-delivered, adaptive, regionally-cached streaming system.

nodes.label: Client ABR Logic
nodes.description: Client ABR Logic: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Streaming API
nodes.description: Streaming API: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: CDN — edge cache
nodes.description: CDN — edge cache: Network-edge component used for global delivery, caching, or traffic acceleration.

nodes.label: Player / Device
nodes.description: Player / Device: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Homepage Service
nodes.description: Homepage Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: DRM License Service
nodes.description: DRM License Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Manifests / Segments
nodes.description: Manifests / Segments: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Catalog / Metadata
nodes.description: Catalog / Metadata: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Open Connect — ISP appliances
nodes.description: Open Connect — ISP appliances: Network-edge component used for global delivery, caching, or traffic acceleration.

nodes.label: Origin Object Store
nodes.description: Origin Object Store: Blob, media, archive, file, or origin storage for large objects.

nodes.label: Packager — HLS/DASH
nodes.description: Packager — HLS/DASH: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Playback Service
nodes.description: Playback Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Recommendation Service
nodes.description: Recommendation Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Renditions — bitrate ladder
nodes.description: Renditions — bitrate ladder: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Playback Sessions
nodes.description: Playback Sessions: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: CDN Steering Service
nodes.description: CDN Steering Service: Network-edge component used for global delivery, caching, or traffic acceleration.

nodes.label: Transcoding Pipeline
nodes.description: Transcoding Pipeline: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Upload Service
nodes.description: Upload Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Entitlement Service
nodes.description: Entitlement Service: Authorizes a playback session against subscription state, regional rights, device rules, and concurrent-stream limits before a license is issued.

nodes.label: Entitlements / Rights
nodes.description: Entitlements / Rights: Authoritative store for subscriptions, content-rights windows by region, and active device/stream sessions.

nodes.label: Transcode Job Store
nodes.description: Transcode Job Store: Tracks per-rendition/per-chunk encoding job state, attempts, worker leases, and publish gating for the ingest pipeline.

nodes.label: QoE Event Stream
nodes.description: QoE Event Stream: Append-only ingest path for playback telemetry events (startup time, rebuffers, bitrate switches, errors) feeding analytics and steering.

nodes.label: Metrics / Observability
nodes.description: Metrics / Observability: Aggregates QoE and infrastructure metrics into dashboards and alerts, and feeds CDN steering decisions.

nodes.label: Regional Origin Shield
nodes.description: Regional Origin Shield: Mid-tier regional cache/fill layer between edge caches and origin that absorbs misses and shields origin from the thundering herd.

functional: Upload source videos and process them for streaming.
Browse a catalog and a personalized homepage.
Play a title on any device, starting quickly and resuming where left off.
Adapt video quality to the viewer's network conditions.
Protect content (DRM) so only entitled users can play.
nonFunctional: Low startup latency and minimal rebuffering during playback.
Massive read/egress bandwidth — video dominates internet traffic.
Global reach with low latency regardless of viewer location.
Cost-efficient delivery — bandwidth is the dominant cost.
High availability of playback; degrade quality rather than stop.
Scale storage for a large catalog x many encodings.

capacity.label: Peak concurrent viewers
capacity.note: Example workload: 60M simultaneous streams at the evening peak. This is the multiplier behind every number below.

capacity.label: Peak egress bandwidth
capacity.note: 60M viewers × ~5 Mbps average encoded bitrate ≈ 300 Tbps. Video dominates internet traffic; this is what CDN offload must absorb.

capacity.label: Segment GETs/sec
capacity.note: Each viewer fetches one 4s segment every 4s, so 60M viewers ≈ 15M GET/sec (call it 12–15M) across the edge fleet (Step 4 ABR is the request driver).

capacity.label: Origin egress after offload
capacity.note: At a 99.5% edge hit ratio, origin still sees ~1.5 Tbps before the regional shield tier (Step 3). This is why tiered caching / origin shield exists.

capacity.label: Playback starts/sec
capacity.note: Each start hits playback + entitlement + license (Step 5), so these are the QPS that size the control plane, not the media path.

capacity.label: Heartbeats/sec
capacity.note: 60M sessions / 30s ≈ 2M writes/sec — fire-and-forget, last-write-wins, never on the hot media path (Step 5).

capacity.label: Manifest + license req/sec
capacity.note: Roughly one per playback start; license issuance is gated by an entitlement check (Step 5).

capacity.label: Ingest & transcode
capacity.note: New content/day × ~8–12 renditions (resolution × codec) ≈ a ~10× storage multiplier per title; bursty worker-fleet compute, time-to-ready target hours not days (Step 2).

capacity.label: Catalog storage
capacity.note: Derive it: catalog ~1M hrs of content × ~8–12 renditions (resolutions × codecs h264/av1) ≈ a ~10× multiplier over a single encode. At a rough ~1–3 GB/hr averaged across the ladder, that is ~tens of PB of renditions per copy; cross-region replication (×2–3 for a region loss not losing the catalog) pushes the durable footprint to ~hundreds of PB. Source masters add more but are cold. This is why renditions live in cheap object storage, not a database.

capacity.label: Worker pool / time-to-ready
capacity.note: A 2-hr 4K title × ~10 renditions of chunked encoding is thousands of chunk-jobs; per-shot/per-title encoding is CPU-heavy. Size the fleet to hit a time-to-ready target (hours, not days) at peak ingest backlog — bursty and elastic, not steady-state — since ingest is rare relative to playback (Step 2).

capacity.label: Startup latency
capacity.note: Time-to-first-frame drives abandonment; modest-start ABR + edge cache hits keep it low (Steps 3–4).

capacity.label: Read:write
capacity.note: Upload/transcode is rare and batched; segment reads are constant and enormous. Segment length is ~2–10s (4s typical) for adaptive streaming.

api.description: Return the streaming manifest (HLS/DASH): the list of bitrate renditions and (encrypted) segment URLs. Device/region-filtered, not secret — it returns only variants this device can decode (codec/HDR/DRM scheme) and only if the title's rights window is open for the region. Segments are cacheable encrypted assets shared across viewers; authorization happens at /v1/playback/start via the DRM license, not here. A blacked-out or unsupported title returns no playable variants (403/empty).

Begin a playback session. Authorizes the request (subscription, regional rights, device, concurrent-stream limit) before issuing a short-lived DRM license. Idempotent on idempotency_key so retries don't open duplicate streams.
Periodic progress update so resume works. Fire-and-forget, last-write-wins, never blocks playback.
Batched QoE/analytics telemetry from the player, appended to the QoE event stream (Step 5). Powers fleet dashboards and feeds CDN steering. Fire-and-forget.
Renew a DRM license for a long session or offline download before it expires. Re-checks entitlement so a lapsed subscription or revoked rights stops future renewals.
Ingest a source video; triggers async transcoding and packaging.

dataModel.name: titles
dataModel.note: Catalog metadata; renditions reference back to a title.

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(received, probing, transcoding, packaging, published, failed, superseded)

fields.name: version
fields.type: int (bump to re-encode / add codec without breaking cached URLs)

dataModel.name: segments
dataModel.note: Short media chunks per rendition, addressed by the manifest; the unit cached at the edge.

dataModel.name: transcode_jobs
dataModel.note: Per-rendition/per-chunk encoding tasks. A title publishes only when every rendition's jobs complete; idempotent retries handle worker crashes and poison chunks.

fields.name: attempts
fields.type: int (retry budget; over budget -> operator alert)

fields.name: output_key
fields.type: varchar (origin pointer for the produced segment)

dataModel.name: playback_sessions
dataModel.note: Tracks an active/last play for resume and QoE.

dataModel.name: entitlements
dataModel.note: What a user is allowed to do: subscription tier/state and the per-account concurrent-stream limit checked at playback start.

dataModel.name: content_rights
dataModel.note: Per-title licensing windows by region — a title may be playable in one region and blacked out in another, with start/end dates.

dataModel.name: device_sessions
dataModel.note: Active streams per user, used to enforce the concurrent-stream limit and to expire/renew DRM licenses.

dataModel.name: qoe_events
dataModel.note: Append-only playback telemetry (startup, rebuffer, bitrate switch, error) keyed by session, partitioned by CDN edge / ISP / title for the operating dashboards.

dataModel.name: user_homepage
dataModel.note: Precomputed personalized rows for fast homepage rendering.

patterns.name: Async transcoding pipeline
patterns.what: Process uploaded video into many renditions off the request path via a work pipeline.
patterns.whenToUse: Expensive, bursty media processing that must not block uploads or playback.

patterns.name: Bitrate ladder
patterns.what: Encode each title at several resolutions/bitrates so the client can switch.
patterns.whenToUse: Streaming to heterogeneous devices and variable networks.

patterns.name: CDN / edge distribution
patterns.what: Serve media segments from caches close to viewers; origin is the fallback.

patterns.name: Adaptive bitrate streaming (HLS/DASH)
patterns.what: A manifest lists renditions; the client picks segments by measured bandwidth.
patterns.whenToUse: Playback over networks whose throughput changes during a session.

patterns.name: Segmented media
patterns.what: Split video into short independently-fetchable chunks.
patterns.whenToUse: Adaptive streaming and efficient edge caching.

patterns.name: Read model / precomputation
patterns.what: Precompute personalized homepage rows so rendering is a fast read.
patterns.whenToUse: Expensive per-user ranking that can't run on every page load.

patterns.name: CDN steering / multi-CDN
patterns.what: Direct each client to the best CDN/edge by health, location, and cost.
patterns.whenToUse: Resilience and cost control across regions and CDN providers.

patterns.name: Pipeline state machine
patterns.what: Track per-job/per-title lifecycle with idempotent retries and publish gating so partial output is never served.
patterns.whenToUse: Long, multi-stage, failure-prone batch processing like media transcoding.

patterns.name: Entitlement / authorization gate
patterns.what: Decide whether a user may play a title (subscription, regional rights, device, stream limit) before issuing a DRM license.
patterns.whenToUse: Protected content where access depends on identity, rights, and policy — not just encryption.

patterns.name: QoE telemetry & observability
patterns.what: Ingest playback quality events to a stream, aggregate into metrics/alerts, and feed them back into steering and ABR.
patterns.whenToUse: When HTTP health is insufficient and quality regressions must be detected and acted on.

patterns.name: Origin shield / tiered caching
patterns.what: A regional mid-tier cache collapses correlated edge misses into a single origin fetch and fans out.
patterns.whenToUse: Very high read fan-out where even a small miss rate would overwhelm origin.

steps.title: 1. Serve the Video File (the baseline)
steps.description: Start naive: store each uploaded video as a file and stream the whole thing from a server on play. It works for one viewer on a fast link, but the math fails fast — just 1M concurrent viewers at ~5 Mbps is ~5 Tbps of origin egress, before any device adaptation. It ignores transcoding for different devices/networks, global latency, and the staggering egress cost — which the rest of the design addresses.
steps.decisionPrompt: One 4K source file, viewers on phones and TVs over varying networks. What's wrong with just serving that file?

concepts.definition: Outbound video bandwidth is the overwhelming cost and load.
concepts.whyItMatters: It forces CDN offload and efficient encoding from the start.

concepts.definition: Viewers differ wildly in screen, codec support, and bandwidth.
concepts.whyItMatters: One file can't serve all; you need multiple renditions.

talkingPoints: Name egress cost, device heterogeneity, and global latency as the forces.
Frame transcoding + CDN as the responses.

traps.trap: Serving one source file to every device from origin
traps.why: Phones waste bandwidth on 4K, weak networks rebuffer, and origin egress cost is ruinous.
traps.instead: Transcode into a bitrate ladder and serve segments from a CDN.

bottlenecks.issue: Origin egress and a single encoding can't serve diverse clients at scale.

strong: Identifies egress cost and device variety immediately.
weak: Treats video like any static file download.

whyNow: The baseline makes transcoding, CDN, and ABR concrete responses to real forces.

view.caption: The player calls the streaming API, which streams the raw video file straight from the origin object store.

steps.title: 2. Upload & Transcoding Pipeline
steps.description: On upload, store the source in an origin object store and kick off an async transcoding pipeline that produces a bitrate ladder (multiple resolutions/bitrates/codecs), then packages each into segmented HLS/DASH with manifests. This is bursty, expensive, and entirely off the playback path.
steps.decisionPrompt: A 2-hour 4K master is uploaded. How do you turn it into something every device can stream, without blocking anything?

concepts.definition: A set of encodings from low to high quality per title.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets the client pick the best quality its network supports.

concepts.definition: Splitting each rendition into short chunks with a manifest.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enables adaptive switching and edge caching of small units.

concepts.definition: An explicit lifecycle per title and per job: received → probing → transcoding → packaging → published, plus failed/superseded.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets the pipeline retry idempotently, gate publishing so partial ladders are never served, and surface poison inputs to operators.

concepts.definition: A title goes 'published' only when all renditions are packaged; re-encodes bump a version rather than mutating cached URLs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents serving an incomplete ladder and lets you add a codec (e.g. AV1) or replace a bad rendition without invalidating cached segments.

options.name: Parallel chunked transcoding (default)
options.description: The source is split into chunks transcoded in parallel by a worker fleet, then packaged into the bitrate ladder and segmented manifests.
pros: Split the source into chunks transcoded in parallel — fast turnaround.
Scales horizontally with workers; bursty cost only at ingest.
cons: Pipeline orchestration and chunk-boundary handling add complexity.

view.caption: The transcoding pipeline splits each title into chunks transcoded in parallel, tracking each chunk in the job store, and writes the completed bitrate-ladder renditions.

options.name: Transcode on the fly at play time
options.description: Segments are encoded on demand as the viewer plays, with no pre-built rendition store, so only watched content is ever transcoded.
pros: No pre-encoding storage; only encode what's watched.
cons: Per-stream CPU on the hot path is expensive and adds latency/rebuffering.
Defeats edge caching — outputs aren't reusable.

view.caption: The player triggers the transcoding pipeline to encode the requested rendition on demand at play time, with no pre-built ladder.

deepDives.title: Pipeline state & publish gating
points: Track per-chunk jobs in a job store (status, attempts, worker lease, output pointer); a lost worker's lease expires and the chunk is reclaimed.
A title only transitions to 'published' once every rendition's chunks complete — partial ladders are never exposed in a manifest.
Poison chunks / corrupt inputs / codec-specific failures exceed a retry budget and raise an operator alert rather than retrying forever.
Re-encoding a title, adding AV1, or replacing a bad rendition bumps a version and writes new URLs, so cached segments are never mutated in place.

deepDives.title: Per-title encoding (cost/quality)
points: A flat ladder over-spends bits on simple content and under-serves complex content; per-title (and per-shot) encoding tunes the ladder to the source.
VMAF (perceptual quality) guides where to place ladder rungs so each bitrate buys real quality.
Cheaper, well-placed renditions reduce both storage multiplier and egress at playback time.

traps.why: Per-viewer CPU on the hot path is costly, slow, and uncacheable.
traps.instead: Pre-transcode the ladder asynchronously at ingest; serve cacheable segments.

traps.why: Weak networks rebuffer and strong ones get sub-par quality.
traps.instead: Produce a full ladder so the client adapts.

traps.trap: Publishing a manifest before the whole ladder is ready
traps.why: Players may select a rendition whose segments don't exist yet and stall or error.
traps.instead: Gate publishing on all renditions completing; expose the title only when 'published'.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The title eventually finishes; partial output isn't served.
failureDrills.mitigation: Chunk-level idempotent jobs retry under a lease; a title goes 'published' only when all renditions complete.

failureDrills.scenario: A specific chunk is a poison input (corrupt frames / unsupported codec path).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The job stops retrying and the title is held back, not published broken.
failureDrills.mitigation: Bounded retry budget; on exhaustion mark the job 'failed' with error detail and alert an operator while the rest of the ladder waits.

strong: Pre-transcodes a ladder via parallel chunked jobs.

recap.before: One source file, no device adaptation.
recap.after: An async pipeline yields a segmented bitrate ladder per title.
recap.newRisk: Storage multiplies by renditions; pipeline orchestration to manage.

whyNow: Playable, adaptable media must exist before distribution and ABR matter.

view.caption: The upload service lands the source in origin; the transcoding pipeline records per-chunk job state, produces the bitrate-ladder renditions, and the packager segments them into HLS/DASH manifests only once every rendition's jobs complete.

flows.name: Upload -> job fan-out -> publish-gated package
flows.note: Ingest stores the source and creates per-chunk jobs; workers claim jobs under a lease and write rendition outputs; the packager runs and the title publishes only once every rendition's jobs are 'done' — all off the playback path.

messages.label: mark done (or retry on crash; alert on poison)
hold publish — no partial ladder exposed

steps.title: 3. CDN / Edge Distribution
steps.description: Serve media segments from CDN edge caches close to viewers; the origin object store is only the fallback for misses. Popular content lives at the edge, slashing latency and origin egress. Manifests point clients at CDN URLs.
steps.decisionPrompt: Most viewers watch a small set of popular titles. How do you avoid serving every byte from origin?

concepts.definition: Caches near users holding popular segments.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns repeated global reads into local hits — latency and cost drop.

concepts.definition: Fraction of traffic the CDN absorbs so origin isn't hit.
concepts.whyItMatters: At streaming scale, origin egress would be unaffordable without high offload.

deepDives.title: Maximizing offload
points: Cache immutable segments aggressively (long TTL) — media doesn't change once published.
Pre-position (pre-warm) popular new releases to edges before launch spikes.
Tiered caching (edge -> regional shield -> origin) so a miss rarely reaches origin.

deepDives.title: Origin offload math & cold titles
points: Even a 99.5% edge hit ratio on ~300 Tbps peak leaves ~1.5 Tbps hitting origin — far too much to serve directly.
A regional origin shield collapses correlated misses: many edges in a region miss the same new segment, but the shield fetches origin once and fans out.
Cold/long-tail titles miss the edge by design; the shield + regional fill keep their origin reads cheap and local.
Pre-warming popular new releases turns a launch-day thundering herd into edge hits before the spike arrives.

traps.trap: Serving segments straight from origin/object store
traps.why: Origin egress at streaming scale is astronomically expensive and far from viewers.
traps.instead: Front everything with a CDN; origin only fills misses.

traps.trap: Short or no cache TTL on immutable segments
traps.why: Constant revalidation defeats the cache and hammers origin.
traps.instead: Treat published segments as immutable with long TTLs; version by URL on change.

failureDrills.scenario: A new release goes viral at launch.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Edges absorb it without melting origin.
failureDrills.mitigation: Pre-warm popular titles to edges; tiered caching shields origin from the thundering herd.

strong: Fronts media with a CDN, caches immutable segments long, pre-warms hits.

recap.after: Edge caches serve popular content locally; origin only fills misses.
recap.newRisk: Cache invalidation/versioning and pre-warming become operational concerns.

whyNow: Distribution is where the bandwidth problem is actually solved.

view.caption: The player reads the manifest then fetches segments from the CDN edge cache; a miss goes to the regional origin shield, which fills from the origin object store and fans out to other edges.

options.name: Managed third-party CDN(s) (default)
options.description: Media segments are served from a commercial CDN's global edge caches, with the origin object store acting only as the miss fallback.
pros: Buy global edge coverage instantly from established providers — no edge hardware to deploy.
Popular segments cache at the edge, slashing viewer latency and origin egress.
Manifests just point at CDN URLs; the origin store is only the miss fallback.
cons: Per-GB egress at video scale is the dominant cost — you pay the CDN's margin forever.
Less control over edge placement and peering than owning the appliances.
A single provider's regional outage affects all viewers routed there (motivates multi-CDN later).

view.caption: The player fetches segments from a managed third-party CDN edge cache, which fills from the origin object store on a miss.

options.name: ISP-embedded appliances (build your own edge)
options.description: You deploy your own caching appliances inside ISP networks and pre-fill popular titles, serving them from within the last mile.
pros: Caching boxes placed inside ISP networks serve popular titles from within the last mile — lowest latency and transit cost.
At Netflix's scale, owning the edge is far cheaper per GB than paying a CDN's egress.
Pre-position (pre-fill) tomorrow's popular titles during off-peak so peak is mostly local hits.
cons: Huge capital and operational investment — only worth it at very high sustained volume.
Requires ISP partnerships and physical logistics to deploy and maintain hardware.
Long-tail/cold titles still fall back to regional origin.

view.caption: The player fetches segments from Open Connect appliances embedded inside ISPs, which are filled from the CDN edge and ultimately the origin.

options.name: P2P / client-assisted delivery
options.description: Viewers in the same region share already-downloaded segments peer-to-peer, with the CDN as a backstop for what peers can't supply.
pros: Viewers in the same region share segments peer-to-peer, offloading bytes from the CDN/origin.
Scales naturally with audience: more concurrent viewers means more upload capacity.
Can dramatically cut egress for a synchronized popular live or new release.
cons: Residential upstream is weak and asymmetric, so a CDN backstop is still required.
DRM, privacy, and ISP data-cap concerns complicate consumer adoption.
Churn and NAT traversal make peer availability unpredictable for steady QoE.

view.caption: The player fetches segments from peer clients in a P2P swarm, falling back to the CDN edge cache when peers can't serve them.

flows.name: Segment fetch: edge hit vs shielded origin miss
flows.note: The client reads the manifest, then fetches segments from the CDN edge; an edge miss goes to the regional origin shield, which collapses correlated misses into a single origin fetch and fans the segment back out — origin is rarely touched directly.

steps.title: 4. Adaptive Bitrate Streaming
steps.description: The client drives quality: it reads the manifest, measures throughput and buffer level, and requests the next segment at the bitrate it can sustain — stepping down to avoid rebuffering, up when the network allows. The server just serves segments; intelligence lives in the player.
steps.decisionPrompt: A viewer's bandwidth drops mid-show. How does playback stay smooth without you doing anything server-side?

concepts.definition: The player chooses each segment's bitrate from the ladder based on measured conditions.
concepts.whyItMatters: Only the client sees real-time buffer/throughput; centralizing this wouldn't scale.

concepts.definition: Use buffer occupancy (not just bandwidth) to decide up/down switches.
concepts.whyItMatters: More stable than bandwidth-only heuristics, which overreact to noise.

deepDives.title: Smooth adaptation
points: Start at a modest bitrate for fast startup, then ramp up as the buffer fills.
Switch down quickly under buffer pressure; switch up cautiously to avoid oscillation.

deepDives.title: Manifest & device compatibility
points: Segment boundaries must align across renditions so the player can switch mid-stream without a gap.
The manifest is filtered to the device's capabilities: codec support (h264 vs AV1), max resolution, HDR, and DRM scheme — a device only sees variants it can actually decode.
Audio and subtitle tracks are separate selectable groups in the manifest, switched independently of video.
Effectively each device class gets its own ladder slice, so a phone isn't offered 4K it can't use.

traps.why: The server can't see each client's live buffer/throughput, and centralizing it doesn't scale.
traps.instead: Let the player run ABR; the server only serves the ladder's segments.

traps.trap: Starting at the highest bitrate for fast 'quality'
traps.why: It delays startup and risks immediate rebuffering on weak links.
traps.instead: Start modest for quick first-frame, then adapt up.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Quality dips but playback doesn't stall.
failureDrills.mitigation: Buffer-based ABR steps down to a sustainable rendition, then recovers.

strong: Puts ABR in the client using buffer + bandwidth; aligns segments for clean switches.

recap.before: Quality was fixed and rebuffering likely on bad networks.
recap.after: The player adapts bitrate per segment for smooth playback.
recap.newRisk: Requires aligned segments and a tuned ABR algorithm.

whyNow: Adaptation is what makes the ladder + CDN actually deliver smooth playback.

view.caption: The player's client-side ABR logic reads the manifest variants and requests the next segment at the bitrate matching current bandwidth from the CDN.

options.name: Client-driven ABR (default)
options.description: The player measures its own throughput and buffer level and requests each next segment at the bitrate it can sustain; the server just serves segments.
pros: The player measures its own throughput and buffer and picks the next segment's bitrate — closest to ground truth.
The server just serves static segments, so delivery stays simple and CDN-cacheable.
Reacts instantly to a mid-show bandwidth drop without any server round trip.
cons: Quality depends on each player's ABR algorithm; clients can behave inconsistently.
Greedy players can compete unfairly for shared bandwidth.
The server has little control over the experience or global optimization.

view.caption: The player's own ABR logic reads the variant list and picks each next segment's bitrate, fetching it from the CDN.

options.name: Server-guided / server-side ABR
options.description: A control service uses session and network telemetry to recommend bitrates per session, steering quality decisions centrally.
pros: A control service uses session and network telemetry to recommend bitrates, enabling fleet-wide optimization.
Can enforce fairness across many sessions sharing a link and protect a stressed CDN edge.
Centralizes A/B testing and policy for quality decisions.
cons: Adds a server hop and a stateful control service on the playback path.
Server estimates lag the true client conditions, so it reacts slower to sudden drops.
Reintroduces server-side state and scaling concerns ABR was meant to avoid.

view.caption: The player reports throughput/buffer to a server ABR controller, which reads the variants and recommends the bitrate the player then fetches from the CDN.

flows.name: Client ABR: measure, then request next segment
flows.note: The player drives quality: it reads the ladder, measures throughput/buffer, and picks the bitrate for the next segment — stepping down under pressure, up cautiously.

steps.title: 5. Playback Sessions, Resume & DRM
steps.description: Manage the watch experience: a playback service authorizes each start against entitlements (subscription, regional rights, device, concurrent-stream limit) before issuing a short-lived DRM license, returns the resume position, and ingests fire-and-forget heartbeats plus richer QoE events. Resume, entitlement, and quality telemetry are app state, separate from media delivery.
steps.decisionPrompt: A user starts a show on their phone and finishes on their TV. How does 'resume' work, and how do you keep it cheap?

concepts.definition: Server state tracking the current/last position and QoE per user+title.
concepts.whyItMatters: Powers resume and analytics without touching the media path.

concepts.definition: A short-lived token authorizing decryption of protected content for an entitled user/device.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps content protected independent of where segments are cached; expiry forces re-authorization for long sessions and offline downloads.

concepts.definition: Before issuing a license: authenticate the user, verify subscription state, regional content rights, device capability, and the concurrent-stream limit.
concepts.whyItMatters: Protection is authorization plus encryption — a license is only issued after the playback service decides the user may actually play this title here, now.

deepDives.title: The playback authorization contract (manifest, segments, license)
points: Default model (used here): segments are cacheable immutable encrypted assets fetched by a shared CDN URL, and authorization lives entirely in the DRM license — only an entitled session gets a license, so segment URLs being broadly fetchable is harmless because the bytes are useless without the key.
This keeps the CDN object key identical for every viewer (maximizing cache hit ratio); per-user authorization never varies the cached object, so it stays cache-safe.

The manifest is device- and region-filtered, not a secret: /v1/titles/{id}/manifest returns only the variants this device can decode (codec/HDR/DRM scheme) and only if the title's content_rights window is open for the region — a blacked-out or unsupported title returns no playable variants.

Optional hardening for higher-value content: playback/start can return a session-scoped or short-lived signed CDN URL (token validated at the edge) instead of a bare manifest URL; the trick is to validate the token without varying the cache key, so segments stay shared and cacheable while casual scraping is curbed.

Either way the boundary is explicit: manifest = device/region-filtered catalog of encrypted segment URLs; license = the per-session authorization that actually unlocks playback; CDN = dumb cacheable byte delivery that does no per-user access control.

deepDives.title: QoE telemetry vs. resume heartbeats
points: Keep two paths: tiny resume heartbeats (last-write-wins into the session store) and richer QoE events (startup time, rebuffers, bitrate switches, errors, CDN edge, ISP, player version) batched to a QoE event stream — never write QoE on the segment hot path.
The QoE stream feeds observability dashboards (startup p95, rebuffer minutes/hour, license-start failures, per-ISP/per-edge breakdown) and, in turn, CDN steering (Step 7).
HTTP 200s are not enough: a silent quality failure (rebuffer ratio spikes for one ISP while requests still succeed) is only visible through QoE metrics.

deepDives.title: Heartbeat path under 2M writes/sec
points: Partition the session store by session_id (or user_id) so the ~2M writes/sec spread evenly across shards with no hot key; each session touches one partition.

Coalesce writes: a player ticks every ~30s, the write is last-write-wins on position, so an in-memory/edge buffer can drop intermediate ticks and persist the newest — losing a heartbeat just means resume is a few seconds stale, which is acceptable.
Fire-and-forget with bounded client-side retry (a couple of attempts, then drop) — a heartbeat must never block or delay playback.
Reap stale sessions via a TTL on device_sessions (e.g. expire after a few missed heartbeats) so abandoned streams free a concurrent-stream slot.

deepDives.title: QoE stream: dedupe, retention, backpressure
points: Events carry an event_id so the stream/consumer can dedupe at-least-once delivery; late events are tolerated by windowed aggregation (watermarks) rather than dropped outright.

Distinguish the stores rather than treating them as one DB: a low-latency keyed session/resume store, an append-only QoE event log (Kafka/Kinesis), real-time aggregates (startup p95, rebuffer ratio by edge/ISP) for alerting/steering, and a columnar warehouse (ClickHouse/BigQuery) for longer-term fleet analysis with a bounded retention window (raw events days–weeks, aggregates much longer).
Sample/minimize: high-volume event types can be sampled, and PII is minimized (coarse geo/ISP, not raw IP) to keep retention cheap and privacy-safe.

Backpressure rule: QoE is best-effort. If the pipeline is degraded, playback continues and the client drops or summarizes low-priority telemetry (keep startup/error, shed verbose switch events) before it ever risks the playback hot path.

traps.trap: Writing every heartbeat synchronously and durably
traps.why: Frequent progress pings at scale would hammer the DB on the hot path.
traps.instead: Fire-and-forget heartbeats; update the session asynchronously with last-write-wins.

traps.trap: Enforcing entitlement only at the CDN/segment layer
traps.why: Cached segments are widely distributed; CDN can't reliably check per-user rights.
traps.instead: Gate decryption with a DRM license issued by the playback service per session.

failureDrills.scenario: The session/heartbeat store is briefly unavailable.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Playback continues; resume may be slightly stale.
failureDrills.mitigation: Heartbeats are best-effort; the player also keeps local position and reconciles later.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Already-licensed sessions keep playing until their license expires; new starts may fail or enter a constrained mode.
failureDrills.mitigation: License TTLs let in-flight playback continue; new starts can fall back to a cached entitlement decision or a degraded (lower-tier) mode, and renew once the service recovers.

failureDrills.scenario: HTTP is healthy (all 200s) but rebuffer ratio spikes for one ISP.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The silent quality regression is detected even though no request is erroring.
failureDrills.mitigation: QoE metrics (rebuffer ratio, startup p95) broken down by ISP/edge alert on the regression; steering shifts that ISP's traffic to a healthier edge.

strong: Keeps heartbeats async/best-effort and separates resume/DRM from delivery.
Gates the license on an explicit entitlement decision (sub, region, device, stream limit), not just encryption.
weak: Relies on CDN for access control.

recap.before: Media played but nothing tracked progress or protected it.
recap.after: Sessions enable resume/QoE; DRM tokens gate playback regardless of caching.
recap.newRisk: Session writes must stay off the hot path; DRM adds a license dependency.

whyNow: A real product needs resume, entitlement, and QoE around the media path.

view.caption: On start the playback service authorizes the request via the entitlement service (subscription, regional rights, device, concurrent-stream limit) before issuing a DRM token; it records progress in the sessions store, ingests fire-and-forget heartbeats, and the player streams batched QoE events to the QoE stream and observability (which Step 7 steering later consumes).

options.name: Dedicated session/progress store (default)
options.description: A playback service writes resume position keyed by user and title to a low-latency store, updated by fire-and-forget heartbeats.
pros: A playback service writes resume position to a low-latency store keyed by user+title, so any device reads the latest position instantly.
Heartbeats update progress fire-and-forget, keeping the write path cheap.
Cleanly separates watch state from media delivery and entitlement.
cons: A read/write store for every active session is a lot of small hot writes to manage.
Cross-device resume is only as fresh as the last heartbeat that landed.
Another stateful service to shard, replicate, and scale.

view.caption: The playback service writes start/heartbeat progress to a dedicated session store and issues a DRM token via the license service.

options.name: Derive resume from a heartbeat event stream
options.description: Heartbeats are appended to an event stream and the latest position is materialized into a view, sharing the pipeline with QoE analytics.
pros: Heartbeats are appended to an event stream and the latest position is materialized — same pipeline powers QoE analytics and recommendations.
Writes are pure appends, which absorb huge heartbeat volume cheaply.
One source of truth for both progress and engagement signals.
cons: Resume reads need a materialized view; the raw stream isn't a point-lookup store.
Eventual consistency: the freshest position may lag the latest heartbeat.
More pipeline machinery (stream + aggregation) than a simple keyed store.

view.caption: The playback service appends heartbeats to an event stream that materializes a resume view, which it reads back on start, plus the DRM token.

options.name: Client-local bookmark (sync opportunistically)
options.description: The player stores its watch position locally and syncs it to the server opportunistically, so single-device resume needs no server read.
pros: The player stores its own position locally, so single-device resume needs no server round trip.
Cheapest possible: no per-session server write on the hot path.
Works offline and through brief connectivity gaps.
cons: Cross-device resume (phone to TV) breaks unless the client syncs to the server anyway.
Lost/cleared client state loses the position entirely.
Reconciling positions across devices becomes a client-side conflict problem.

view.caption: The player saves resume position to a client-local bookmark and syncs it opportunistically to the playback service, which still issues DRM tokens.

flows.name: Playback start: authorize, license, then stream QoE
flows.note: Start authorizes the request (subscription, regional rights, device, concurrent-stream limit) before issuing a short-lived DRM license and returning the resume position. Entitlement failures are explicit (not-entitled / stream-limit) rather than silent. Once playing, the player batches fire-and-forget QoE events off the hot path.

messages.label: authorize: sub, region rights, device, stream limit
batched QoE events (startup, rebuffer, switch) — fire-and-forget

steps.title: 6. Catalog, Homepage & Recommendations
steps.description: Browsing is its own read-heavy system. Catalog metadata is cached/CDN-served; the personalized homepage is precomputed per user (rows of ranked titles) by an offline recommendation pipeline, so loading the homepage is a fast read, not a live ranking.
steps.decisionPrompt: Ranking thousands of titles per user is expensive. How is the homepage instant?

concepts.definition: Per-user ranked rows materialized offline and read at page load.
concepts.whyItMatters: Live ranking per visit wouldn't meet latency at scale.

concepts.definition: Batch/stream jobs compute rankings from viewing history.
concepts.whyItMatters: Heavy ML/ranking runs off the request path and refreshes periodically.

traps.trap: Ranking the catalog live on every homepage load
traps.why: Per-visit ranking of a large catalog blows the latency budget for a read-heavy page.
traps.instead: Precompute per-user rows offline; the page load just reads them.

traps.why: Artwork and metadata are also high-volume reads.
traps.instead: Cache/CDN catalog assets like any other static content.

strong: Precomputes homepage rows offline and CDN-caches catalog assets.

recap.after: Precomputed personalized rows + cached catalog make browsing fast.
recap.newRisk: Recommendation freshness vs. cost; precompute lag is acceptable.

whyNow: Discovery sits in front of playback and is its own read-scaling problem.

view.caption: The player browses the homepage service, which assembles catalog metadata with precomputed rows from the recommendation service, with metadata cached at the CDN.

options.name: Offline-precomputed personalized rows (default)
options.description: A batch recommendation pipeline ranks titles per user ahead of time into stored rows, so loading the homepage is a fast read.
pros: A batch recommendation pipeline ranks titles per user ahead of time, so loading the homepage is a fast read, not a live ranking.
Heavy ranking runs off the request path, decoupling cost from page-load latency.
Catalog metadata is cached/CDN-served, so browsing is cheap and globally fast.
cons: Rows are as stale as the last pipeline run — new signals (just-watched) aren't reflected until recompute.
Precomputing rows for every user (including inactive ones) wastes compute and storage.
Reacting to in-session behavior needs a separate freshness mechanism.

view.caption: The recommendation service precomputes personalized rows offline and pushes them to the homepage service, which combines them with catalog metadata.

options.name: Online (real-time) ranking per request
options.description: Titles are ranked at request time by a model-serving call, so the homepage reflects the latest signals on every page load.
pros: Ranks titles at request time, so the homepage reflects the latest signals (just-finished show, trending now).
No precompute storage for inactive users — you only rank when someone actually loads the page.
Easier to incorporate real-time context (device, time of day) directly.
cons: Ranking thousands of titles per request is expensive and adds page-load latency.
A model-serving dependency now sits on the read path at homepage scale.
Hard to keep within a fast-page budget for every user at peak.

view.caption: The homepage service ranks titles live through an online ranking service per browse request rather than reading precomputed rows.

options.name: Hybrid: precomputed rows + real-time rerank
options.description: Precomputed candidate rows are served fast, then the top few are lightly reranked with fresh in-session signals on the request path.
pros: Serve precomputed candidate rows fast, then lightly rerank the top with fresh in-session signals.
Most of the cost stays offline; only a cheap rerank touches the request path.
Captures both stable preferences and immediate context (what you just watched).
cons: Two ranking systems (batch + online rerank) to build and keep consistent.
The rerank still adds some latency and a serving dependency.
Blending offline and online scores is a tuning problem of its own.

view.caption: The homepage service takes precomputed recommendation rows and reranks the top rows in real time before returning them.

deepDives.title: Freshness tiers without recomputing on every load
points: Offline tier: a batch pipeline computes per-user candidate rows from viewing history and refreshes them periodically (hours) — most of the page is served straight from this precomputed read.

Online tier (optional): only the top few items are lightly reranked at request time with fresh in-session signals (just-watched, time of day), so the page stays fast while reflecting immediate context.
Catalog/artwork are immutable-ish reads cached at the CDN; a catalog change (new title, updated artwork) versions the asset URL so a precompute lag never serves a broken card.
The right boundary for a streaming interview: keep this read-fast and degradation-tolerant — it is not a full recommendation-system design.

failureDrills.scenario: The recommendation pipeline is stale or unavailable.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The homepage still renders and playback is unaffected; rows fall back to generic/popular/editorial content.
failureDrills.mitigation: Serve the last good precomputed rows; if those are missing, fall back to popular/trending or editorial rows. Browse is a read-only convenience path — it never blocks playback, which depends only on the manifest/CDN/playback path.

flows.name: Homepage load: precomputed rows + cached catalog
flows.note: Browsing is a fast read: the homepage service reads precomputed personalized rows and hydrates them with catalog metadata/artwork served from cache/CDN. If recommendations are stale/unavailable it degrades to popular rows — playback is never on this path.

messages.label: fall back to popular / editorial rows

steps.title: 7. Scaling & Resilience (multi-CDN, regional)
steps.description: Operate globally and resiliently: steer each client to the best CDN/edge (including ISP-embedded appliances like Open Connect) by location, health, and cost; replicate origin across regions; and degrade quality rather than fail when an edge or region is impaired.
steps.decisionPrompt: A whole CDN region degrades during peak. How do clients keep streaming?

concepts.definition: A service directing clients to the optimal CDN/edge per request.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enables failover, load balancing, and cost control across providers.
Cuts transit cost and latency to the last mile for popular content.

options.name: Multi-CDN steering + ISP appliances (default)
options.description: A steering service routes each client to the healthiest, cheapest edge across multiple CDNs and ISP appliances by location and health.
pros: Failover across CDNs/regions; pick the healthiest, cheapest edge.
ISP appliances slash last-mile cost/latency for hits.
cons: Steering service and multi-CDN ops are complex; consistent measurement needed.

view.caption: The CDN steering service picks an edge using health and cost signals across multiple CDNs.

options.name: Single CDN provider
options.description: All viewers are served from one CDN provider with a single integration and no cross-provider steering.
pros: Simplest; one integration.
cons: That provider's outage takes you down; no cost/perf arbitrage.

view.caption: The player fetches directly from a single CDN edge cache with no steering layer.

traps.why: Its outage is your outage, with no failover or cost leverage.
traps.instead: Steer across multiple CDNs/edges and fail over on health signals.

traps.trap: Failing playback when the preferred edge is down
traps.why: Viewers see errors when a lower-quality stream was still possible.
traps.instead: Re-steer and let ABR degrade quality so playback continues.

strong: Uses multi-CDN steering, ISP appliances, and graceful degradation.

recap.before: One CDN, no failover, fragile under regional trouble.
recap.after: Health/cost-aware steering across CDNs + ISP appliances, degrading gracefully.
recap.newRisk: Steering and multi-CDN measurement/ops are ongoing complexity.

whyNow: Global resilience and cost control are the final levers at streaming scale.

view.caption: The player asks the CDN steering service where to fetch; steering reads health/QoE signals from observability and routes to the best CDN edge or Open Connect appliance, with the regional shield and origin behind them as fill.

deepDives.title: Degrade, don't fail
points: On edge/region trouble, steer clients to the next-best edge transparently.
If only lower-bitrate segments are reachable, let ABR drop quality rather than stall.
Replicate origin across regions (with a regional shield) so a region loss doesn't lose the catalog or melt a single origin.

deepDives.title: How steering actually works
points: Steering can be DNS-based (coarse, cached by resolvers, slow to fail over), manifest-based (segment URLs rewritten per session — fast, fine-grained), client-config-based (the player holds a ranked endpoint list it cycles on error), or a hybrid; manifest + client-list is the most responsive.
Inputs are geo/latency, real-time edge health and QoE (rebuffer/error rates by edge and ISP), and per-edge cost.
Steering returns a prioritized list, not a single endpoint, so the player can retry the next endpoint without another round trip.
Config changes are risky: roll them out gradually and watch QoE, because a bad steering map can stampede traffic onto a cold or overloaded edge.

flows.name: Edge selection with fallback
flows.note: This flow owns endpoint selection only — the player asks steering where to fetch and steering combines geo, real-time health/QoE, and cost into a prioritized endpoint list; the player tries the best edge and falls back down the list on error, degrading quality (via ABR) rather than failing. The fill path behind a chosen edge (edge -> regional shield -> origin) is owned by Step 3 and the step view shows it; on a cold miss the chosen edge fills via the shield exactly as in Step 3 before returning bytes.

messages.label: retry next endpoint (ABR may drop quality)

failureDrills.scenario: A whole CDN region degrades during peak.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Clients re-steer to healthy edges/CDNs; playback continues, possibly at lower quality.
failureDrills.mitigation: Health-aware steering returns alternate endpoints; the player fails over down its list and ABR degrades quality instead of stalling.

failureDrills.scenario: Origin shield in a region is overloaded by correlated misses.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Origin is shielded; misses are collapsed rather than amplified.
failureDrills.mitigation: Request coalescing at the shield (single origin fetch fanned out), tiered fill, and pre-warming popular content before the spike.

failureDrills.scenario: A bad steering config rolls out and stampedes a cold edge.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Blast radius is limited and the change is rolled back quickly.
failureDrills.mitigation: Gradual rollout with QoE guardrails and fast rollback; clients keep a sane fallback endpoint list independent of the latest config.

failureDrills.scenario: A tentpole launch's pre-warm misses some edges.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The thundering herd doesn't reach origin directly.
failureDrills.mitigation: Tiered caching + shield coalescing absorb the cold fill; steering biases traffic toward warmed edges while the rest fill.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: uploads land in origin object storage and an async pipeline (tracked by a transcode job store) transcodes a bitrate ladder packaged into segmented HLS/DASH, publishing only complete ladders; manifests point clients at CDN edges (and ISP appliances) that cache immutable segments, with a regional origin shield and origin behind them as fill; a CDN steering service picks the best edge using health/QoE signals; the player runs adaptive bitrate; a playback service authorizes each start against entitlements/rights before issuing a DRM license and tracks sessions/resume; player and session QoE telemetry flow into observability, which feeds steering; a precomputed recommendation pipeline feeds a fast personalized homepage.

view.caption: Uploads transcode (with job-state tracking) into a rendition ladder packaged as manifests; players browse via the homepage and recommendations, start playback after an entitlement check and DRM license, and use steering plus ABR to fetch segments from the CDN, Open Connect, shield, or origin; QoE telemetry feeds observability and steering.

functional.how: Async transcoding pipeline -> bitrate ladder -> segmented packaging.

functional.requirement: Play and resume on any device
functional.how: Playback sessions store position; manifests + CDN serve segments.
Client ABR selects segments from the ladder.

Segments are cacheable encrypted assets; an entitlement check (subscription, regional rights, device, concurrent-stream limit) gates a short-lived DRM license per session, and the manifest is device/region-filtered. Authorization lives in the license, not the CDN, so segments stay cache-safe; license expiry forces re-authorization.

nonFunctional.how: CDN/edge + ISP appliances absorb the vast majority of traffic.
Caches close to viewers; steering to the best edge.
High origin offload, immutable long-TTL caching, ISP appliances.
QoE/health-aware multi-CDN steering with endpoint fallback + ABR quality degradation instead of failure; origin replicated regionally behind a shield.
Origin object store holds source + renditions; regional replication.

tradeoff: Multi-CDN + your own ISP appliances give the best cost/resilience at sustained streaming scale, but owning edge hardware is a huge capital and ops commitment.
Managed CDNs are instant global coverage you rent; steering across several of them buys failover and price arbitrage without owning anything.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: High edge offload makes most origin-egress capacity planning unnecessary — origin only fills misses behind the shield.

tradeoff: Object storage is the natural home for immutable source masters, renditions, and segments — cheap, durable, and CDN-friendly.
Cross-region replication of origin is what lets a region loss not lose the catalog.
A managed transcode/orchestration service handles the encode ladder, retries, and job state for you, but a self-hosted FFmpeg + workflow engine gives full control over per-title/per-shot encoding.
Either way the job-state machine (leases, idempotency, publish gating) is the part that must be correct.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed transcoder removes most hand-built chunk-orchestration and packaging glue.

tradeoff: An append-only log absorbs huge heartbeat/QoE volume cheaply and feeds resume materialization, QoE dashboards, and recommendation signals from one pipeline.
Self-hosted Kafka is maximal control; managed streams remove broker ops.
Resume/session state is a high-write, key-lookup workload that fits a wide-column or KV store; entitlements/rights are smaller, relational-ish, and read-heavy.
A managed autoscaling store removes most manual sharding for the hot session writes.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed autoscaling NoSQL store removes the need to hand-shard the heartbeat write path.

tradeoff: QoE analytics needs both real-time metrics (startup p95, rebuffer ratio by ISP/edge) for alerting/steering and a columnar warehouse for deeper fleet analysis.
HTTP health alone misses silent quality regressions — QoE is a first-class operating signal, not a nice-to-have.
Multi-DRM (Widevine/PlayReady/FairPlay) is effectively mandatory because device platforms each mandate their own scheme; a license-issuing service brokers all three.
DRM gates decryption, but the entitlement decision (sub, region, stream limit) is yours and sits in front of license issuance.

say: Restate it: ingest, transcode, distribute, and adaptively stream video globally.
List functional (upload, browse, play/resume, adapt, DRM) and non-functional (startup, egress, latency, cost, availability).
Name the forces: egress cost, device/network heterogeneity, and global latency.
Pick a workload: e.g. 60M concurrent × ~5 Mbps ≈ 300 Tbps peak egress; at 99.5% edge hit ratio origin still sees ~1.5 Tbps, which motivates the shield.
Derive segment GETs/sec (≈12M/s on 4s segments), playback starts (~50–100k/s), and heartbeats (~2M/s every 30s).
Sketch manifest, playback start (entitlement → license), heartbeat, QoE events, and ingest.
Start naive, then the transcoding pipeline + ladder, then CDN distribution, then client ABR.
Add playback sessions/resume/DRM and the precomputed homepage.
Go deep where pushed: transcoding pipeline, CDN offload, or ABR.
Multi-CDN steering + ISP appliances + graceful degradation; regional origin.
Summarize requirements and name the dominant force (egress cost / availability).

expectations: Knows video needs transcoding into multiple qualities and a CDN, not raw file serving.
Understands playback should resume and adapt.
Designs an async chunked transcoding pipeline producing a segmented ladder.
Puts ABR in the client and maximizes CDN offload with immutable caching.
Separates playback sessions/DRM from media delivery.
Uses multi-CDN steering and ISP appliances for cost and resilience, degrading gracefully.
Reasons about origin offload ratio, pre-warming, and regional replication.
Quantifies the bandwidth-cost / quality / availability tradeoffs.

followUps: How would you support live streaming (low-latency) alongside video-on-demand?
How do you handle per-title encoding optimization (different ladders for animation vs. action)?
How would you do global content launch / pre-positioning for a tentpole release?
How do you measure and act on QoE (rebuffer ratio, startup time) across the fleet?
How would you enforce concurrent-stream limits and offline downloads with DRM?
How do you roll out a new codec (e.g. AV1) without re-encoding everything at once?

links.groupDescription: Primary Netflix references and papers on CDN topology, Open Connect appliances, edge fill, routing, and origin offload.
links.title: Netflix Open Connect
links.why: Shows the public shape of Netflix-owned CDN deployment, ISP partnerships, embedded appliances, and peering options.

links.groupDescription: Primary Netflix references and papers on CDN topology, Open Connect appliances, edge fill, routing, and origin offload.
links.title: Open Connect Overview
links.why: Explains OCAs, AWS control-plane steering, authorization handoff, nightly fills, monitoring, and popularity-aware placement.

links.groupDescription: Primary Netflix references and papers on CDN topology, Open Connect appliances, edge fill, routing, and origin offload.
links.title: Open Connect Everywhere: A Glimpse at the Internet Ecosystem through the Lens of the Netflix CDN
links.why: Independent measurement paper useful for understanding how ISP peering and IXP placement change CDN topology.

links.groupDescription: How video assets become bitrate ladders, how codec rollouts are staged, and how quality is measured.
links.title: Per-Title Encode Optimization
links.why: Explains why a fixed bitrate ladder wastes bandwidth and how per-title analysis picks better resolution/bitrate pairs.

links.groupDescription: How video assets become bitrate ladders, how codec rollouts are staged, and how quality is measured.
links.title: Dynamic Optimizer - a Perceptual Video Encoding Optimization Framework
links.why: Deepens the transcoding step with per-shot optimization, perceptual metrics, and production constraints beyond per-title ladders.

links.groupDescription: How video assets become bitrate ladders, how codec rollouts are staged, and how quality is measured.
links.title: Optimized shot-based encodes for 4K: Now streaming!
links.why: Shows codec/quality migration work for premium bitstreams and the rollout concerns behind changing an existing catalog.

links.groupDescription: How video assets become bitrate ladders, how codec rollouts are staged, and how quality is measured.
links.title: Toward a Practical Perceptual Video Quality Metric
links.why: Connects video quality measurement to encoding validation, QoE tradeoffs, and automated ladder decisions.

links.groupDescription: How video assets become bitrate ladders, how codec rollouts are staged, and how quality is measured.
links.title: Netflix VMAF
links.why: Provides the production-grade open-source implementation behind VMAF-based codec and encode quality evaluation.

links.groupDescription: How video assets become bitrate ladders, how codec rollouts are staged, and how quality is measured.
links.title: Bringing AV1 Streaming to Netflix Members' TVs
links.why: Useful for discussing codec rollout, device capability gating, monitoring, and incremental migration of playback formats.

links.groupDescription: Standards, browser APIs, and ABR algorithms that shape HLS/DASH playback behavior.
links.title: HTTP Live Streaming
links.why: Reference for HLS manifests, segments, CDN delivery, adaptation behavior, tooling, and device ecosystem constraints.

links.groupDescription: Standards, browser APIs, and ABR algorithms that shape HLS/DASH playback behavior.
links.title: HLS Authoring Specification for Apple Devices
links.why: Details packaging and stream-authoring constraints that affect bitrate ladders, renditions, captions, validation, and device support.

links.groupDescription: Standards, browser APIs, and ABR algorithms that shape HLS/DASH playback behavior.
links.title: DASH-IF Interoperability Guidelines v5
links.why: Reference for MPEG-DASH interoperability, CMAF mapping, content protection, on-demand service guidance, and client/server contracts.

links.groupDescription: Standards, browser APIs, and ABR algorithms that shape HLS/DASH playback behavior.
links.title: Media Source Extensions
links.why: Defines the browser buffering API that lets web players append media segments and implement adaptive playback.

links.groupDescription: DRM, realtime events, observability, and stream-processing references for operating playback at scale.
links.title: Encrypted Media Extensions
links.why: Defines the browser API contract between playback code and content decryption modules for DRM-protected streams.

links.groupDescription: Standards, browser APIs, and ABR algorithms that shape HLS/DASH playback behavior.
links.title: BolaRule
links.why: Concrete player documentation for a buffer-based ABR rule and the practical knobs exposed by a reference DASH player.

links.groupDescription: Standards, browser APIs, and ABR algorithms that shape HLS/DASH playback behavior.
links.title: BOLA: Near-Optimal Bitrate Adaptation for Online Videos
links.why: Foundational ABR paper that frames bitrate selection as a buffer-aware utility optimization problem.

links.groupDescription: Standards, browser APIs, and ABR algorithms that shape HLS/DASH playback behavior.
links.title: Neural Adaptive Video Streaming with Pensieve
links.why: Shows the tradeoff space for learned ABR policies and QoE reward design beyond hand-written heuristics.

links.groupDescription: DRM, realtime events, observability, and stream-processing references for operating playback at scale.
links.title: Mantis
links.why: Netflix open-source stream processing platform relevant to realtime playback-health monitoring and operational event analysis.

links.groupDescription: DRM, realtime events, observability, and stream-processing references for operating playback at scale.
links.title: Keystone Real-time Stream Processing Platform
links.why: Explains Netflix-scale event ingestion and routing, useful for QoE events, recommendation facts, and async media pipelines.

links.groupDescription: DRM, realtime events, observability, and stream-processing references for operating playback at scale.
links.title: Improved Alerting with Atlas Streaming Eval
links.why: Shows why high-cardinality, near-realtime alert evaluation matters for playback-health and regional degradation detection.

links.groupDescription: DRM, realtime events, observability, and stream-processing references for operating playback at scale.
links.title: Apache Kafka Design
links.why: Explains partitions, logs, consumer groups, and replication patterns used by async transcoding and telemetry pipelines.

links.groupDescription: How Netflix and related research personalize discovery surfaces and evaluate changes.
links.title: The Netflix Recommender System: Algorithms, Business Value, and Innovation
links.why: Primary paper from Netflix authors on homepage ranking, search, offline evaluation, and A/B testing tradeoffs.

links.groupDescription: How Netflix and related research personalize discovery surfaces and evaluate changes.
links.title: Foundation Model for Personalized Recommendation
links.why: Modern Netflix case study on scaling recommendation models, interaction tokenization, cold start, embeddings, and inference latency.

links.groupDescription: How Netflix and related research personalize discovery surfaces and evaluate changes.
links.title: Artwork Personalization at Netflix
links.why: Shows a production personalization problem adjacent to ranking: evidence selection, contextual bandits, cacheability, and A/B testing.

links.groupDescription: How Netflix and related research personalize discovery surfaces and evaluate changes.
links.title: It's All A/Bout Testing: The Netflix Experimentation Platform
links.why: Explains allocation, latency, and instrumentation concerns behind safely evaluating changes to recommendations and playback.

links.groupDescription: Other shipped media platforms and CDN products that expose useful implementation tradeoffs.
links.title: Warehouse-Scale Video Acceleration: Co-design and Deployment in the Wild
links.why: YouTube/Google paper on dedicated video transcoding hardware, useful for contrasting software pipelines with custom acceleration.

links.groupDescription: Other shipped media platforms and CDN products that expose useful implementation tradeoffs.
links.title: Live Video Transmuxing/Transcoding: FFmpeg vs TwitchTranscoder, Part I
links.why: Production media pipeline case study that clarifies transmuxing, transcoding, HLS delivery, and operational cost tradeoffs.

links.groupDescription: Other shipped media platforms and CDN products that expose useful implementation tradeoffs.
links.title: Welcome to Adaptive Media Delivery
links.why: Shows CDN product capabilities for ABR streaming, edge caching, origin failover, and secure media delivery.
